name: Castor
description: Castor, also known as Kastor, is a prominent figure in Greek mythology, best known as one of the Dioscuri, the twin brothers of Helen of Troy and Clytemnestra. His parentage is complex, with some accounts identifying Tyndareus, the king of Sparta, as his father, while others attribute his paternity to Zeus, making him a demigod. Castor is typically depicted as a skilled horseman and warrior, renowned for his prowess in taming horses. He and his brother Pollux (Polydeuces), who was typically considered the immortal son of Zeus, were inseparable and exemplified fraternal devotion. Their bond was so strong that when Castor was mortal and slain, Pollux shared his immortality with him, allowing them to spend alternate days in Olympus and the Underworld, a testament to their enduring love and loyalty. Castor's association with horsemanship and athletics contributed to his veneration in athletic contests and military training.
subtitle: Tamer of Horses

keyMyths.title: The Death of Castor and Pollux's Shared Immortality
keyMyths.description: The most famous myth associated with Castor is his death. He and Pollux, along with their cousins Idas and Lynceus, engaged in a cattle-rustling raid. A dispute arose, leading to a fatal battle. Castor, being mortal, was slain by Idas. Grief-stricken, Pollux begged Zeus to allow him to share his immortality with his brother. Zeus granted his wish, and the twins were allowed to spend alternate days in Olympus and the Underworld, symbolizing their eternal bond and devotion, a poignant tale of brotherly love and sacrifice.

keyMyths.title: The Abduction of the Daughters of Leucippus
keyMyths.description: Castor and Pollux are also known for abducting Phoebe and Hilaeira, the daughters of Leucippus. These women were already betrothed to Idas and Lynceus, further fueling the conflict that would eventually lead to Castor's death. The abduction is often depicted in art and literature and highlights the Dioscuri's active involvement in heroic exploits and their role as protectors and avengers. This event contributed to the animosity between the cousins and set the stage for the tragic events that followed.

keyMyths.title: Patronage of Sailors
keyMyths.description: Castor and Pollux were revered as protectors of sailors. It was believed that their presence during storms was indicated by St. Elmo's fire, a luminous plasma discharge seen on ships' masts. The appearance of St. Elmo's fire was considered a good omen, signifying that the storm would soon abate and that the sailors would be safe under the Dioscuri's watchful eyes. This association with seafaring solidified their role as benevolent deities who offered protection and guidance to those who ventured out on the seas.
